title: Tibouchina: The Princess Flower Genus
excerpt: A guide to Tibouchina, one of the most widely cultivated ornamental genera in the Melastomataceae family.

title: Melastomataceae: A Complete Family Overview
excerpt: An introduction to the Melastomataceae family — where it fits in the plant kingdom and why it matters.

excerpt: Light, soil, water, and pruning basics for keeping melastome plants healthy at home.

title: Miconia: A Genus of Giants and Cautionary Tales
excerpt: Miconia is one of the largest genera in the plant kingdom — and one species became a notorious invasive.

title: Medinilla: The Showy Epiphyte of the Family
excerpt: Medinilla brings hanging, chandelier-like flower clusters to a family better known for shrubs.

title: Melastoma malabathricum: The Singapore Rhododendron
excerpt: Despite the common name, this widespread Asian shrub has nothing to do with true rhododendrons.

title: Memecylon: The Overlooked Melastome Relative
excerpt: A genus that shows how messy plant classification can get, even for close relatives.

title: Rhexia: North America's Native Meadow Beauties
excerpt: One of the few Melastomataceae genera native to temperate North America.

title: Blakea: Understanding a Neotropical Epiphytic Genus
excerpt: A cloud-forest genus that spends much of its life growing on other trees.

title: Plant Family Classification: How Melastomataceae Fits In
excerpt: A plain-English look at how botanists organize plants into orders, families, and genera.

title: Reading a Botanical Name: A Beginner's Guide
excerpt: What genus, species, and author citations actually mean when you see a scientific name.

title: How New Plant Species Get Described
excerpt: Melastomataceae species are still being formally described. Here's what that process involves.

title: Buzz Pollination: How Bees Unlock Melastome Flowers
excerpt: Many melastomes hide their pollen behind a mechanism only certain bees can open.

title: Why Some Melastomes Are Considered Invasive Weeds
excerpt: The same traits that make melastomes easy to grow can make a few species hard to control.

title: Melastomataceae in Traditional Medicine: What the Research Says
excerpt: Several melastomes have long histories in folk medicine. Here's a grounded look at the evidence.

title: Repotting a Melastome: When and How
excerpt: Signs your plant has outgrown its pot, and how to repot without stressing it.

title: Propagating Tibouchina from Cuttings
excerpt: A step-by-step method for growing new Tibouchina plants from a healthy parent.

title: Common Pests on Melastome Houseplants
excerpt: What to look for, and how to deal with the handful of pests that actually bother these plants.

title: Winter Care for Tender Melastomes
excerpt: How to get frost-sensitive melastomes through a cold season without losing them.

title: Choosing Soil and Fertilizer for Acid-Loving Melastomes
excerpt: Why the wrong soil pH quietly stunts melastomes, and how to get it right.

title: Dissotis: The African Melastomes You've Probably Never Grown
excerpt: Most melastome growers know Tibouchina and Miconia. Dissotis, Africa's contribution to the family, deserves a look too.

title: Sonerila: Tiny Melastomes Built for Terrariums
excerpt: Most melastomes think big. Sonerila is the genus that stayed small, and it fits life under glass better than almost anything else in the family.

title: Bertolonia: Jewel-Leaf Melastomes for Low Light
excerpt: Bertolonia trades big flowers for some of the most striking foliage in the entire family — and it thrives in the low light most houseplants hate.

title: Heterocentron: The Spanish Shawl and Its Trailing Cousins
excerpt: Most of this family grows upright. Heterocentron macrostachyum, the Spanish shawl, does the opposite — and it's one of the easiest melastomes to grow outdoors.

title: Osbeckia: Old World Melastomes with a Long Folk History
excerpt: Osbeckia has been used in traditional medicine across Asia and Africa for generations. Here's what the genus actually looks like botanically.

title: Centradenia: A Shade-Tolerant Melastome Worth Knowing
excerpt: Centradenia does something few melastomes manage: it flowers reliably in part shade, which makes it useful in spots where Tibouchina simply won't perform.

title: Monochaetum: A High-Altitude Andean Melastome
excerpt: While most melastomes are lowland tropical plants, Monochaetum has adapted to cool, high-elevation cloud forest — and it needs different treatment because of it.

title: Clidemia hirta: The Melastome That Became a Global Weed
excerpt: Clidemia hirta is a case study in how an unremarkable-looking shrub becomes one of the most disruptive invasive plants on tropical islands.

title: Tococa and the Ant-Plants of the Melastome Family
excerpt: A handful of Amazonian melastomes grow hollow leaf pouches specifically to house colonies of ants. It's one of the stranger relationships in the plant kingdom.

title: Bird-Dispersed Melastome Fruits: Who Eats What
excerpt: The purple berries on a wild melastome aren't just decorative — they're a deliberate strategy, and different bird species respond to them differently.

title: A Short History of Melastomataceae in Ornamental Horticulture
excerpt: Tibouchina didn't become a garden staple by accident. Here's a brief look at how this family moved from herbarium specimens to nursery shelves.

title: Pleroma vs Tibouchina: Why Your Plant Tag Might Be Wrong
excerpt: A 2019 taxonomic revision moved most of the species gardeners know as Tibouchina into a different genus, Pleroma. Nurseries haven't caught up, and it shows.

title: Reading Leaf Venation: The One Trick That Helps ID a Melastome
excerpt: You can identify a plant as a likely melastome from across the room, without seeing a single flower — if you know what to look for in the leaf veins.

title: Poricidal Anthers Explained: The Pepper-Shaker Flower
excerpt: Melastome flowers don't release their pollen the way most flowers do. They shake it out through tiny pores — and only certain bees know how to trigger it.

title: Why Melastomataceae Cluster in the Tropics
excerpt: With a small handful of exceptions, this entire family is tropical. That's not a coincidence — it traces back to where and when the family originated.

title: Growing Tibouchina from Seed
excerpt: Cuttings are faster, but growing Tibouchina from seed is cheap, produces more plants, and isn't as hard as it sounds if you get the basics right.

title: Pruning Tibouchina for Shape and Repeat Bloom
excerpt: An unpruned Tibouchina gets leggy and sparse fast. A little cutting at the right time of year keeps it dense, shapely, and flowering harder.

title: How Much Light Do Melastomes Actually Need
excerpt: Light needs vary a lot across this family — a rule that works for Tibouchina will fail badly on Medinilla or Bertolonia. Here's how to tell what your plant actually wants.

title: Fixing Yellow Leaves on a Potted Melastome
excerpt: Yellowing leaves are one of the most common complaints with potted melastomes — and one of the most commonly misdiagnosed. Here's how to actually narrow it down.

title: Watering Melastomes: The Mistakes That Actually Kill Them
excerpt: More potted melastomes die from watering mistakes than from pests, cold, or bad light combined. Most of it comes down to a couple of habits worth breaking.
